text: Recently I posted a robotic right colectomy and I got some very welcomed feedback about my approach and technique and I was really looking forward to recording another one and on my next one really focusing on more meticulously dissecting out the root of the ileocolic pedicle and even individually dissecting out the artery and vein and ligating those separately. but then this happened. So this is a very high BMI patient, greater than forty, it's a male, and just a lot of intra-abdominal fat. I got in there and noticed that his transverse colon, in spite of lifting up the greater momentum above it, was encased or cocooned in this kind of fat here. I wasn't even able to see the transverse colon initially, so spent the first, you know, several minutes of the case, trying to free up these atypical adhesions and expose the transverse colon and just trying to restore normal anatomy. But after doing this for a while and evaluating his mesentery and anatomy for a medial to lateral approach, which is my typical approach, I felt that this gentleman would be a good candidate for a lateral to medial approach just to make it easier on myself and to have better visualization when I finally got to take the pedicle. So I put together this quick video just to kind of illustrate pivoting based on the patient's anatomy and needing to feel comfortable approaching these cases in different ways. So I hope you enjoy and again As always, the feedback is very much welcome. So after getting a feel of what it would be like to try to approach this medial to lateral through that very thick mesentery, this is where I changed the approach and decided to go lateral to medial for the majority of the case here. I will kind of speed through this dissection.

reasonable person may ask well if this is an easier approach if this is the fallback well then why isn't this the standard approach and into that I would say the medial to lateral approach feels easier to me at this point for most patients most patients with more favorable anatomy with maybe thinner mesentery without so much scarring on that medial mesentery That does feel easier and quicker in the dissection. But just with certain patients' body habitus, I think this lateral to medial approach is a good fallback. And I can feel a little bit more safe than just trying to directly dissect through that thick mesentery down to the iliocolic pedicle. So I went from lateral to medial for quite a while here. Didn't quite reach all the way to the duodenum. You'll see that a bit later. But at this point, said, okay, there's some lateral to medial. Let's go back and free up this greater momentum from the transverse colon and better define that anatomy and then carry out the hepatic flexor mobilization with the vessel sealer and meet those two dissection planes.

The surgeon completes the hepatic flexure mobilization, connecting two dissection planes to reach the proximal transverse colon at the distal resection margin. The duodenum is identified and the dissection continues on top of it, aiming to complete the lateral to medial aspect. A Raytech is placed on the duodenum to guide the dissection through the thick mesentery. The surgeon reaches the root of the ileocolic vessels, cleaning the ileocolic vein from the ileal side through the ileal mesentery, and prepares to select the distal resection margin through the colonic mesentery.

So listen, I will be the first to admit that this is not the prettiest dissection. I went into this case or I guess I went into the idea of my next right colectomy that I would post to be a case where I took the criticisms of my previous post and really worked hard on more cleanly dissecting out the root of the ileocolic vessels, the right colic vessel, individually taking these after scissor dissection. But I found that this just was not the right case for that. I'm continuing to learn, continuing to try to improve with every case. Heck, that's why I'm posting these things is so that I can learn from your criticisms and your ideas and especially from watching your surgeries as well. For this kind of patient with this thick of a mesentery, I felt that changing the approach was what was best for me and the patient. I felt that taking his ileocolic vessels with this white load was going to make me feel most secure. And ultimately, I am pretty satisfied, you know, with his outcome and with the result of the operation. I do wonder, however, if I am in the minority of surgeons out there that would have felt like he or she had to change their approach to this case to feel more comfortable with it. Also about running into those strange cocoon-like adhesions around the transverse colon if you've seen that and how you dealt with that or what that might be from. And also about, you know, skeletonizing the ileocolic vessels through, you know, a two-inch thick mesentery. All these things, this won't be the last patient that I have with this type of body habitus. Heck, it probably won't be the last one in the next couple of weeks.
